_provenance.what: Gold set for the AI CARD-CHECK evaluation (rubric challenge 7f): 50 MCAT-relevant Q&A pairs with known-correct answers, used to check LLM-generated flashcards for factual and pedagogical quality.

source_sections.3.1: Synthesis of Biological Macromolecules (dehydration synthesis, hydrolysis)
source_sections.3.4: Proteins (amino acids, peptide bonds, structure levels)

_provenance.record: (id, content_area, question, correct_answer). correct_answer is the KNOWN-CORRECT ground truth drawn from the named source; it is the only reference the card-checker judges a generated card against.
_provenance.how_built: Facts hand-transcribed from the named OpenStax chapter and cross-checked. These are genuinely correct, unambiguous, exam-relevant facts. This is the honest version of a 'gold set with known-correct answers' -- NOT harvested from a model, and NOT tuned against any generated card.

gold.question: In a dehydration synthesis reaction, what small molecule is released as two monomers are joined?
What type of reaction breaks a polymer into monomers by adding a water molecule across the bond?
What is the general term for the small repeating subunits that link together to form a polymer?
What are the four major classes of biological macromolecules?
gold.correct_answer: Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids

gold.question: What class of biological catalyst is required to drive dehydration and hydrolysis reactions in cells?
What is the general empirical formula for a carbohydrate?
What is the molecular formula of glucose?
What is the simplest type of carbohydrate, a single sugar unit such as glucose or fructose?
Which disaccharide (common table sugar) forms from glucose and fructose?
Which disaccharide forms from glucose and galactose?
Which disaccharide forms from two glucose molecules?
What is the name of the covalent bond that joins two monosaccharides?
What polysaccharide is the primary storage form of glucose in plants?
What polysaccharide is the primary storage form of glucose in animals, stored in liver and muscle?
What structural polysaccharide makes up plant cell walls?
What structural polysaccharide forms the exoskeletons of arthropods and the cell walls of fungi?
Glucose, galactose, and fructose all share the formula C6H12O6 but differ in structure; what is this relationship called?
Are lipids generally hydrophilic or hydrophobic?
A triglyceride (fat) is composed of glycerol bonded to how many fatty acid chains?
What type of covalent bond links a fatty acid to glycerol in a fat?
What structural feature defines a saturated fatty acid?
gold.correct_answer: It has no carbon-carbon double bonds (carbons are saturated with hydrogen)

gold.question: What structural feature defines an unsaturated fatty acid?
gold.correct_answer: It has one or more carbon-carbon double bonds

gold.question: What class of lipid is the major structural component of cell membranes?
A phospholipid has a hydrophilic head and what kind of tails, making it amphipathic?
What structural feature is characteristic of all steroids?
Which steroid is a component of animal cell membranes and a precursor to steroid hormones?
At room temperature, are saturated fats typically solid or liquid?
Industrially hydrogenated unsaturated fats with double bonds in the trans configuration are called what?
What waterproof lipids form protective coatings on feathers, leaves, and skin?
What is the monomer (building block) of a protein?
How many different amino acids are commonly found in proteins?
What is the name of the covalent bond that links two amino acids together?
Which part of an amino acid varies between the 20 amino acids and determines its chemical properties?
What does the primary structure of a protein refer to?
gold.correct_answer: The linear sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain

gold.question: What are the two main types of protein secondary structure?
What type of bonding holds together a protein's secondary structure?
gold.correct_answer: Hydrogen bonds (between atoms of the polypeptide backbone)

gold.question: What does the tertiary structure of a protein describe?
gold.correct_answer: The overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide

gold.question: What structural level exists only in proteins made of more than one polypeptide subunit?
What is the term for the loss of a protein's three-dimensional shape (and function) due to heat or pH changes?
What class of macromolecule are enzymes (which act as biological catalysts)?
Hemoglobin, made of four polypeptide subunits, is a classic example of which level of protein structure?
What are the two types of nucleic acids?
What is the monomer of a nucleic acid?
What are the three components of a nucleotide?
gold.correct_answer: A nitrogenous base, a pentose (five-carbon) sugar, and a phosphate group

gold.question: What five-carbon sugar is found in DNA?
What are the four nitrogenous bases found in DNA?
Which nitrogenous base replaces thymine in RNA?
Which two nitrogenous bases are purines?
In DNA, adenine pairs with which base, and guanine pairs with which base?
gold.correct_answer: Adenine pairs with thymine, and guanine pairs with cytosine

gold.question: Is DNA typically single-stranded or double-stranded?
